Theory

  • Theory Profile
  • Project Compass

Theory | Chapter 1 | 1 min read | Home

Core Theory

1. Design Thesis (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:9)

  • Navigation, not prediction. The central design decision in Wayfinder is that proof search should be spatial navigation through structured mathematical space rather than sequential token prediction.

7. Connection to Mutation Theory (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:349)

  • The Mutation Theory corpus provides formal guarantees applicable to Wayfinder's navigational search.
  • These connections are structural correspondences, not loose analogies — theorem proving is the ML application domain most naturally aligned with formal learning theory (exact verification oracle, well-defined state space, decomposable structure).

Overview (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:10)

  • The experimental pipeline is designed so that a single set of experiments tests all three claims.

Abstract (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:11)

  • We propose a novel approach to neural theorem proving that treats proof search as navigation through structured semantic space rather than token prediction.
  • Navigational claim. A structured semantic network — where mathematical entities (lemmas, tactics, proof states) are positioned along orthogonal signed dimensions and connected through a shared anchor dictionary — enables premise selection and proof search via deterministic symbolic operations (IDF-weighted set intersection, Bellman-Ford spreading activation, multiplicative bank alignment) that are faster, more auditable, and more precise than dense embedding retrieval.
  • Architectural claim. A hybrid continuous-ternary architecture — with a continuous goal encoder, a learned information bottleneck, and a ternary {-1, 0, +1} navigational decoder — produces directional coordinates in proof space rather than vocabulary indices.

1.4 Research Questions (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:71)

  • This work addresses five interconnected questions:
  • 3. Search efficiency (Q3): Does separating the neural network (runs once per proof state) from the search engine (runs deterministic symbolic operations) enable faster proof search with equivalent or better success rates?
  • 5. Convergence (Q5): Do the formal guarantees from Mutation Theory (trajectory monotonicity, phase transitions, fixed-point partitions) apply to this navigational search process, providing formal convergence bounds?

1.5 Scope and Stance (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:85)

  • Our contribution is architectural: demonstrating that a small model (sub-100M trainable parameters) with structured navigation can achieve competitive results by spending compute on symbolic search rather than neural inference.

2.1 Proof Search as Spatial Navigation (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:99)

  • We propose a fundamental reframing: proof search is navigation through a structured mathematical space, not sequential token prediction.
  • Consequence 2: Auditable search. Every premise retrieval traces back to specific shared anchors and bank alignments. "This lemma scored 0.87 because it shares the rare anchors monotone-convergence and finset-sum with the current goal, and its DOMAIN bank position aligns with the goal's." Compare to dense retrieval: "the embedding similarity was 0.87" (uninspectable).

2.2 Signed Semantic Banks for Mathematical Entities (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:115)

  • We define six orthogonal proof banks — signed dimensions with zero states placed at the mode of mathematical activity:

2.7 Connections to Mutation Theory (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:326)

  • The Mutation Theory corpus (200+ Lean 4 theorems across 7 phases) provides formal guarantees that connect directly to navigational proof search.
  • Of all application domains for machine learning, mathematical theorem proving is arguably the most naturally aligned with formal learning theory — the state space is well-defined, the verification oracle is exact, and the mathematical structure of the domain mirrors the mathematical structure of the learning theory.
  • Research needed: Existing work on proof search termination and Lyapunov-style arguments in interactive theorem provers (e.g., well-founded recursion in Lean itself, termination metrics in Isabelle's Sledgehammer) may provide tighter bounds than the general Mutation Theory guarantees.
  • The three-signal multiplicative structure is not an arbitrary design choice; it mirrors the three-way partition that Mutation Theory proves is exhaustive and disjoint.
  • This provides a formal lower bound on the premise_candidates parameter: if a theorem's specification complexity is 4, retrieving 3 premises is provably insufficient.

2.8 Why Theorem Proving is Uniquely Aligned with Learning Theory (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:358)

  • The Mutation Theory connections above are stronger than typical "ML meets formal methods" claims, for a structural reason: mathematical theorem proving is the domain where learning theory's assumptions are most naturally satisfied.
  • The Lean kernel is that oracle — it provides binary, deterministic, zero-noise feedback on whether a tactic application is correct.
  • The implication is practical: formal learning theory should not be treated as an aspirational connection but as an engineering tool for this domain.

6.13 Mutation Theory (Vinaik, 2025-2026; formalized in Lean 4) (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:851)

  • 200+ Lean 4 theorems formalizing specification complexity, exact learning, teaching dimension, decomposition, trajectory convergence, and phase transitions. Connection to Wayfinder: Provides formal convergence guarantees.

7.1 The Intelligence Budget Argument (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:859)

  • The key insight driving Wayfinder is about where to spend intelligence.
  • The model must encode all mathematical knowledge in its weights because search is parametric.

Core Theory (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:8)

  • Design Thesis (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:9) - Navigation, not prediction.* The central design decision in Wayfinder is that proof search should be spatial navigation through structured mathematical space rather than sequential token prediction*.
  • Connection to Mutation Theory** (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:349) - The Mutation Theory corpus provides formal guarantees applicable to Wayfinder's navigational search. - These connections are structural correspondences, not loose analogies — theorem proving is the ML application domain most naturally aligned with formal learning theory (exact verification oracle, well-defined state space, decomposable structure).
  • Overview (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:10) - The experimental pipeline is designed so that a single set of experiments tests all three claims.
  • 1.5 Scope and Stance (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:85) - Our contribution is architectural: demonstrating that a small model (sub-100M trainable parameters) with structured navigation can achieve competitive results by spending compute on symbolic search rather than neural inference.
  • 2.2 Signed Semantic Banks for Mathematical Entities (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:115) - We define six orthogonal proof banks — signed dimensions with zero states placed at the mode of mathematical activity:

Problem Solving (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:84)

  • 2.1 Proof Banks (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:23) - Why signed hierarchies instead of categories: A categorical "domain" field with values {algebra, topology, analysis} can't express proximity. - Signed positions give gradient scoring — a linear algebra lemma (DOMAIN = 0, between concrete and abstract) scores better against an algebra query than a topology lemma, without binary mismatch.
  • The Name* (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:591) - They navigated by understanding the structure* of the space, not by memorizing routes.
  • 5.1 Enriched Proof Network (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:702) - If navigational retrieval underperforms dense retrieval: - Add LLM-based anchor extraction (Tier 3) for subtle mathematical properties - Add cross-namespace links from proof co-occurrence analysis - Experiment with finer-grained bank positions (depth 3-4 instead of 1-2)
  • 3.9 Deterministic Lowering (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:664) - This is the existing lowering.py module, adapted to accept navigational resolution output instead of tier tokens.
  • 6.2 AlphaProof (DeepMind, 2024) (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:801) - Critically, the value function predicts remaining tactics to completion, not binary provability — because all proven states have probability 1.0, destroying the training signal. - Backpropagates the value of the hardest branch rather than the product. Design influence: Soft critic targets (distance, not binary) adopted directly.

Alignment (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:191)

  • 2.1 Proof Banks (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:23) - Why signed hierarchies instead of categories: A categorical "domain" field with values {algebra, topology, analysis} can't express proximity.
  • 1.1 The Navigation Problem in Proof Search (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:29) - Every competitive neural theorem prover uses the same fundamental approach: encode the proof state as a dense vector, predict the next tactic token from a vocabulary, and embed the prediction in a tree search (BFS, MCTS, or best-first).
  • 1.5 Scope and Stance (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:85) - Non-goal: Beating DeepSeek-Prover-V2's 88.9% on MiniF2F. - Our contribution is architectural: demonstrating that a small model (sub-100M trainable parameters) with structured navigation can achieve competitive results by spending compute on symbolic search rather than neural inference.
  • 2.8 Why Theorem Proving is Uniquely Aligned with Learning Theory (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:358) - The Lean kernel is that oracle — it provides binary, deterministic, zero-noise feedback on whether a tactic application is correct.
  • 3.6 Proof Navigator (Ternary Decoder) (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:527) - The core innovation. - Unlike a traditional decoder that classifies into a tactic vocabulary, the Proof Navigator outputs directions in proof space. - AlphaProof similarly trains its value function to predict remaining tactic count rather than binary provability, because all proven states have probability 1.0, destroying the training signal.

Problem Solving

1. Design Thesis (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:9)

  • Navigation, not prediction. The central design decision in Wayfinder is that proof search should be spatial navigation through structured mathematical space rather than sequential token prediction.
  • If dense retrieval consistently outperforms structured navigation for premise selection (Phase 2.2 of the plan), the navigational paradigm is wrong for this domain.
  • If tactic classification outperforms navigational coordinates (Phase 4.4 ablation), the ternary decoder design is wrong.

2.1 Proof Banks (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:23)

  • Why signed hierarchies instead of categories: A categorical "domain" field with values {algebra, topology, analysis} can't express proximity.
  • Signed positions give gradient scoring — a linear algebra lemma (DOMAIN = 0, between concrete and abstract) scores better against an algebra query than a topology lemma, without binary mismatch.

10. The Name (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:591)

  • They navigated by understanding the structure of the space, not by memorizing routes.

Hardware & Distribution Strategy (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:30)

  • Adapted from the proven ModelAtlas pipeline pattern: standalone workers, JSONL interchange, scp-deployable, --resume support.
  • Distribution pattern: - CPU-heavy extraction/analysis scripts are standalone workers with zero Wayfinder src/ imports - All data exchange via JSONL (one JSON object per line, UTF-8) - Workers support --resume (read output, build skip set, append) - File sync via parsync (parallel SSH, resumable, chunked) instead of scp - Merge results back into the main DB on the laptop
  • Key parallelism: While the laptop runs encoder evaluation (Phase 0.6, GPU-bound), macpro and homebridge run proof network extraction and anchor gap analysis (Phase 0.1-0.5, CPU-only).

0.3 Implement Proof Network Extraction Pipeline (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:118)

  • What: Deterministic extraction of bank positions and anchors from Lean type information.

1.4 Full Pipeline Smoke Test (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:368)

  • Stop/go: If the full pipeline doesn't produce gradients on all components, debug before proceeding.

2.0 Literature Review: Lean4 Proof Search Algorithms (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:388)

  • Key findings already incorporated: - AlphaProof uses remaining-tactic-count as value target (adopted for critic head) - HTPS: soft critic targets >> hard binary (adopted) - LeanProgress: proof history gives 13-point improvement (adopted) - BFS-Prover: best-first search competitive with MCTS when policy is strong - No system uses knowledge graph navigation for proof search (validates novelty) - LeanHammer hybrid achieves 37.3% (hammer integration adopted)
  • Time: 2-3 days concurrent with Phase 1/2 implementation.

5.1 Enriched Proof Network (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:702)

  • If navigational retrieval underperforms dense retrieval: - Add LLM-based anchor extraction (Tier 3) for subtle mathematical properties - Add cross-namespace links from proof co-occurrence analysis - Experiment with finer-grained bank positions (depth 3-4 instead of 1-2)

Abstract (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:11)

  • We propose a novel approach to neural theorem proving that treats proof search as navigation through structured semantic space rather than token prediction.
  • Navigational claim. A structured semantic network — where mathematical entities (lemmas, tactics, proof states) are positioned along orthogonal signed dimensions and connected through a shared anchor dictionary — enables premise selection and proof search via deterministic symbolic operations (IDF-weighted set intersection, Bellman-Ford spreading activation, multiplicative bank alignment) that are faster, more auditable, and more precise than dense embedding retrieval.
  • Architectural claim. A hybrid continuous-ternary architecture — with a continuous goal encoder, a learned information bottleneck, and a ternary {-1, 0, +1} navigational decoder — produces directional coordinates in proof space rather than vocabulary indices.
  • Epistemological claim. The architecture produces measurable learning trajectories, enabling Process-Aware Benchmarking (PAB) to evaluate how it learns to navigate, not just whether proofs close.

1.4 Research Questions (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:71)

  • 2. Architectural (Q2): Can a ternary {-1, 0, +1} decoder that produces navigational coordinates (directions in proof space) outperform classification-based tactic prediction, by leveraging the semantic network for resolution?
  • 3. Search efficiency (Q3): Does separating the neural network (runs once per proof state) from the search engine (runs deterministic symbolic operations) enable faster proof search with equivalent or better success rates?
  • 4. Process (Q4): Does the architecture's learning trajectory satisfy PAB's quality criteria?
  • 5. Convergence (Q5): Do the formal guarantees from Mutation Theory (trajectory monotonicity, phase transitions, fixed-point partitions) apply to this navigational search process, providing formal convergence bounds?

1.5 Scope and Stance (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:85)

  • Our contribution is architectural: demonstrating that a small model (sub-100M trainable parameters) with structured navigation can achieve competitive results by spending compute on symbolic search rather than neural inference.

2.4 Premise Retrieval as Structured Navigation (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:190)

  • 1. Bank alignment. For each bank where the goal has a position, score the candidate lemma's alignment: - Same direction: 1.0 (fully aligned) - At zero: 0.5 (neutral) - Opposite direction: 1 / (1 + |distance|) (decaying penalty) - Multiplicative across banks — a lemma that matches DOMAIN but opposes STRUCTURE gets penalized.
  • Why multiplicative scoring matters for premises: In dense retrieval, a lemma about natural numbers that happens to have similar embedding structure to a topology proof can score highly — the embedding conflates all dimensions.
  • With multiplicative scoring, if the DOMAIN bank doesn't align, the score goes to zero.
  • A zero in any bank kills the total score.
  • The confidence-weighted variant is theoretically most principled: if the GoalAnalyzer's bank head outputs a softmax distribution where the winning class has only 40% probability (uncertain), that bank should influence the final score less than one where the winning class has 95% probability.

2.9 PAC Learning and Process-Aware Evaluation (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:374)

  • Process-Aware Benchmarking (PAB), introduced by Pal (2025), evaluates how models learn, not just what they achieve.
  • PAB tracks how this estimate improves during training — a model that learns accurate progress prediction early has a better internal model of proof structure than one that predicts well only on familiar theorem types.

3.6 Proof Navigator (Ternary Decoder) (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:527)

  • The core innovation.
  • Unlike a traditional decoder that classifies into a tactic vocabulary, the Proof Navigator outputs directions in proof space.
  • Continuous heads (standard linear, on top of ternary hidden layers): - anchor_logits ∈ ℝ^|anchors|: Which semantic labels should the next tactic/premises have - progress ∈ ℝ^1: Estimated remaining proof steps (MSE loss against ground truth).
  • AlphaProof similarly trains its value function to predict remaining tactic count rather than binary provability, because all proven states have probability 1.0, destroying the training signal.
  • Where: - L_nav: Cross-entropy on navigational directions (ternary classification, 6 banks, summed) - L_anchor: Binary cross-entropy on anchor predictions - L_progress: MSE on remaining-step prediction - L_critic: MSE on normalized distance-to-completion (soft target, NOT binary BCE — per HTPS finding)

3.9 Deterministic Lowering (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:664)

  • This is the existing lowering.py module, adapted to accept navigational resolution output instead of tier tokens.

6.2 AlphaProof (DeepMind, 2024) (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:801)

  • Critically, the value function predicts remaining tactics to completion, not binary provability — because all proven states have probability 1.0, destroying the training signal.
  • Backpropagates the value of the hardest branch rather than the product. Design influence: Soft critic targets (distance, not binary) adopted directly.

6.8 LeanHammer (2025) and the Hammer Tradition (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:825)

  • Magnushammer's two-stage SELECT+RERANK pipeline achieves 59.5% on PISA vs Sledgehammer's 38.3%.
  • Hammer complementarity. The broader hammer tradition (Sledgehammer for Isabelle, CoqHammer for Coq, LeanHammer for Lean) translates ITP goals into first-order logic for external ATP solvers.
  • Hammers dominate the AUTOMATION = -1 region of proof space (fully decidable, translatable to first-order logic).
  • Integration point: when the navigator predicts AUTOMATION = -1 for a goal, delegate directly to LeanHammer/Aesop rather than attempting navigational resolution.

Core Theory (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:8)

  • Design Thesis (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:9) - Navigation, not prediction.* The central design decision in Wayfinder is that proof search should be spatial navigation through structured mathematical space rather than sequential token prediction*.
  • Connection to Mutation Theory** (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:349) - The Mutation Theory corpus provides formal guarantees applicable to Wayfinder's navigational search. - These connections are structural correspondences, not loose analogies — theorem proving is the ML application domain most naturally aligned with formal learning theory (exact verification oracle, well-defined state space, decomposable structure).
  • Overview (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:10) - The experimental pipeline is designed so that a single set of experiments tests all three claims.
  • 1.5 Scope and Stance (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:85) - Our contribution is architectural: demonstrating that a small model (sub-100M trainable parameters) with structured navigation can achieve competitive results by spending compute on symbolic search rather than neural inference.
  • 6.13 Mutation Theory (Vinaik, 2025-2026; formalized in Lean 4) (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:851) - 200+ Lean 4 theorems formalizing specification complexity, exact learning, teaching dimension, decomposition, trajectory convergence, and phase transitions. Connection to Wayfinder: Provides formal convergence guarantees.

Problem Solving (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:84)

  • 2.1 Proof Banks (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:23) - Why signed hierarchies instead of categories: A categorical "domain" field with values {algebra, topology, analysis} can't express proximity. - Signed positions give gradient scoring — a linear algebra lemma (DOMAIN = 0, between concrete and abstract) scores better against an algebra query than a topology lemma, without binary mismatch.
  • The Name* (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:591) - They navigated by understanding the structure* of the space, not by memorizing routes.
  • 0.3 Implement Proof Network Extraction Pipeline (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:118) - What: Deterministic extraction of bank positions and anchors from Lean type information.
  • 1.4 Full Pipeline Smoke Test (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:368) - Stop/go: If the full pipeline doesn't produce gradients on all components, debug before proceeding.
  • 5.1 Enriched Proof Network (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:702) - If navigational retrieval underperforms dense retrieval: - Add LLM-based anchor extraction (Tier 3) for subtle mathematical properties - Add cross-namespace links from proof co-occurrence analysis - Experiment with finer-grained bank positions (depth 3-4 instead of 1-2)

Alignment (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:191)

  • 2.1 Proof Banks (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:23) - Why signed hierarchies instead of categories: A categorical "domain" field with values {algebra, topology, analysis} can't express proximity.
  • 1.1 The Navigation Problem in Proof Search (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:29) - Every competitive neural theorem prover uses the same fundamental approach: encode the proof state as a dense vector, predict the next tactic token from a vocabulary, and embed the prediction in a tree search (BFS, MCTS, or best-first).
  • 1.5 Scope and Stance (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:85) - Non-goal: Beating DeepSeek-Prover-V2's 88.9% on MiniF2F. - Our contribution is architectural: demonstrating that a small model (sub-100M trainable parameters) with structured navigation can achieve competitive results by spending compute on symbolic search rather than neural inference.
  • 2.8 Why Theorem Proving is Uniquely Aligned with Learning Theory (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:358) - The Lean kernel is that oracle — it provides binary, deterministic, zero-noise feedback on whether a tactic application is correct.
  • 3.6 Proof Navigator (Ternary Decoder) (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:527) - The core innovation. - Unlike a traditional decoder that classifies into a tactic vocabulary, the Proof Navigator outputs directions in proof space. - AlphaProof similarly trains its value function to predict remaining tactic count rather than binary provability, because all proven states have probability 1.0, destroying the training signal.

Architecture (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:282)

  • 8.0 Three-Lane Verification Architecture (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:399) - Lane A is the core search loop (proof_search.py → lean_interface.py).
  • 8.1 Navigational Modules (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:479) - Core functions: navigate(), spread(), get_anchor_set(), compute_idf(), batch_get_positions(). - Provides try_tactic(goal_state, tactic_text) -> TacticResult with success/failure and new goal states.
  • 3.4 Lane B: Axle Proof Audit Integration (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:607) - For successful Lane A proofs: auditor.verify() provides authoritative confirmation.
  • Experiment Cycle 2: Retrieval Comparison (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Results.md:192) - Purpose: Stream 1 core question -- does navigational retrieval match or exceed dense retrieval?
  • Per-Ablation PAB Comparison (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Results.md:339) - Key findings: - Navigation thesis (1 vs 2): ____ - Architecture thesis (1 vs 3): ____ - Ternary thesis (1 vs 6): ____ - 6-bank thesis (1 vs 13): ____ - Spreading thesis (1 vs 4): ____

Alignment

1. Design Thesis (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:9)

  • Navigation, not prediction. The central design decision in Wayfinder is that proof search should be spatial navigation through structured mathematical space rather than sequential token prediction.

2.1 Proof Banks (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:23)

  • Why signed hierarchies instead of categories: A categorical "domain" field with values {algebra, topology, analysis} can't express proximity.

2.2 Anchor Dictionary (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:40)

  • For each failure (correct premise not in top-16), identify what anchors would have connected goal to premise.

Hardware & Distribution Strategy (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:30)

  • Distribution pattern: - CPU-heavy extraction/analysis scripts are standalone workers with zero Wayfinder src/ imports - All data exchange via JSONL (one JSON object per line, UTF-8) - Workers support --resume (read output, build skip set, append) - File sync via parsync (parallel SSH, resumable, chunked) instead of scp - Merge results back into the main DB on the laptop
  • Key parallelism: While the laptop runs encoder evaluation (Phase 0.6, GPU-bound), macpro and homebridge run proof network extraction and anchor gap analysis (Phase 0.1-0.5, CPU-only).

0.5 Anchor Gap Analysis (Iterative) (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:218)

  • What: Validate that the proof network can actually retrieve correct premises before any training begins.

5.1 Enriched Proof Network (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:702)

  • If navigational retrieval underperforms dense retrieval: - Add LLM-based anchor extraction (Tier 3) for subtle mathematical properties - Add cross-namespace links from proof co-occurrence analysis - Experiment with finer-grained bank positions (depth 3-4 instead of 1-2)

Abstract (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:11)

  • We propose a novel approach to neural theorem proving that treats proof search as navigation through structured semantic space rather than token prediction.
  • Navigational claim. A structured semantic network — where mathematical entities (lemmas, tactics, proof states) are positioned along orthogonal signed dimensions and connected through a shared anchor dictionary — enables premise selection and proof search via deterministic symbolic operations (IDF-weighted set intersection, Bellman-Ford spreading activation, multiplicative bank alignment) that are faster, more auditable, and more precise than dense embedding retrieval.
  • Architectural claim. A hybrid continuous-ternary architecture — with a continuous goal encoder, a learned information bottleneck, and a ternary {-1, 0, +1} navigational decoder — produces directional coordinates in proof space rather than vocabulary indices.

1.1 The Navigation Problem in Proof Search (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:29)

  • Every competitive neural theorem prover uses the same fundamental approach: encode the proof state as a dense vector, predict the next tactic token from a vocabulary, and embed the prediction in a tree search (BFS, MCTS, or best-first).

1.5 Scope and Stance (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:85)

  • Non-goal: Beating DeepSeek-Prover-V2's 88.9% on MiniF2F.
  • Our contribution is architectural: demonstrating that a small model (sub-100M trainable parameters) with structured navigation can achieve competitive results by spending compute on symbolic search rather than neural inference.

2.4 Premise Retrieval as Structured Navigation (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:190)

  • 1. Bank alignment. For each bank where the goal has a position, score the candidate lemma's alignment: - Same direction: 1.0 (fully aligned) - At zero: 0.5 (neutral) - Opposite direction: 1 / (1 + |distance|) (decaying penalty) - Multiplicative across banks — a lemma that matches DOMAIN but opposes STRUCTURE gets penalized.
  • When bank positions are noisy (and they will be, especially early), this means a misclassified STRUCTURE position can exclude correct premises that match perfectly on every other dimension.
  • LeanDojo's extraction already provides this accessibility information.
  • Four indexed queries instead of 100k dot products.

Resolution via navigate(): (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:259)

  • External validation — activation steering (arXiv 2502.15507). Recent work on activation steering for tactic prediction found that "the LLM is capable of predicting the correct tactic; however, it faces challenges in ranking it appropriately." Steering toward the right reasoning category improves tactic selection.
  • The key finding: argument prediction for abstracted tactics has the highest impact on success.
  • This is partially by design — the anchor logits provide fine-grained disambiguation.

2.8 Why Theorem Proving is Uniquely Aligned with Learning Theory (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:358)

  • The Lean kernel is that oracle — it provides binary, deterministic, zero-noise feedback on whether a tactic application is correct.

3.6 Proof Navigator (Ternary Decoder) (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:527)

  • The core innovation.
  • Unlike a traditional decoder that classifies into a tactic vocabulary, the Proof Navigator outputs directions in proof space.
  • AlphaProof similarly trains its value function to predict remaining tactic count rather than binary provability, because all proven states have probability 1.0, destroying the training signal.

3.9 Deterministic Lowering (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:664)

  • This is the existing lowering.py module, adapted to accept navigational resolution output instead of tier tokens.

6.2 AlphaProof (DeepMind, 2024) (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:801)

  • Critically, the value function predicts remaining tactics to completion, not binary provability — because all proven states have probability 1.0, destroying the training signal.
  • Backpropagates the value of the hardest branch rather than the product. Design influence: Soft critic targets (distance, not binary) adopted directly.

6.8 LeanHammer (2025) and the Hammer Tradition (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:825)

  • Integration point: when the navigator predicts AUTOMATION = -1 for a goal, delegate directly to LeanHammer/Aesop rather than attempting navigational resolution.

Core Theory (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:8)

  • Design Thesis (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:9) - Navigation, not prediction.* The central design decision in Wayfinder is that proof search should be spatial navigation through structured mathematical space rather than sequential token prediction*.
  • Connection to Mutation Theory** (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:349) - The Mutation Theory corpus provides formal guarantees applicable to Wayfinder's navigational search. - These connections are structural correspondences, not loose analogies — theorem proving is the ML application domain most naturally aligned with formal learning theory (exact verification oracle, well-defined state space, decomposable structure).
  • Overview (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:10) - The experimental pipeline is designed so that a single set of experiments tests all three claims.
  • 1.5 Scope and Stance (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:85) - Our contribution is architectural: demonstrating that a small model (sub-100M trainable parameters) with structured navigation can achieve competitive results by spending compute on symbolic search rather than neural inference.
  • 6.13 Mutation Theory (Vinaik, 2025-2026; formalized in Lean 4) (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:851) - 200+ Lean 4 theorems formalizing specification complexity, exact learning, teaching dimension, decomposition, trajectory convergence, and phase transitions. Connection to Wayfinder: Provides formal convergence guarantees.

Problem Solving (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:84)

  • 2.1 Proof Banks (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:23) - Why signed hierarchies instead of categories: A categorical "domain" field with values {algebra, topology, analysis} can't express proximity. - Signed positions give gradient scoring — a linear algebra lemma (DOMAIN = 0, between concrete and abstract) scores better against an algebra query than a topology lemma, without binary mismatch.
  • The Name* (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:591) - They navigated by understanding the structure* of the space, not by memorizing routes.
  • 5.1 Enriched Proof Network (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:702) - If navigational retrieval underperforms dense retrieval: - Add LLM-based anchor extraction (Tier 3) for subtle mathematical properties - Add cross-namespace links from proof co-occurrence analysis - Experiment with finer-grained bank positions (depth 3-4 instead of 1-2)
  • 3.9 Deterministic Lowering (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:664) - This is the existing lowering.py module, adapted to accept navigational resolution output instead of tier tokens.
  • 6.2 AlphaProof (DeepMind, 2024) (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:801) - Critically, the value function predicts remaining tactics to completion, not binary provability — because all proven states have probability 1.0, destroying the training signal. - Backpropagates the value of the hardest branch rather than the product. Design influence: Soft critic targets (distance, not binary) adopted directly.

Alignment (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:191)

  • 2.1 Proof Banks (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:23) - Why signed hierarchies instead of categories: A categorical "domain" field with values {algebra, topology, analysis} can't express proximity.
  • 2.2 Anchor Dictionary (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:40) - For each failure (correct premise not in top-16), identify what anchors would have connected goal to premise.
  • 0.5 Anchor Gap Analysis (Iterative) (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:218) - What: Validate that the proof network can actually retrieve correct premises before any training begins.
  • 1.1 The Navigation Problem in Proof Search (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:29) - Every competitive neural theorem prover uses the same fundamental approach: encode the proof state as a dense vector, predict the next tactic token from a vocabulary, and embed the prediction in a tree search (BFS, MCTS, or best-first).
  • 1.5 Scope and Stance (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:85) - Non-goal: Beating DeepSeek-Prover-V2's 88.9% on MiniF2F. - Our contribution is architectural: demonstrating that a small model (sub-100M trainable parameters) with structured navigation can achieve competitive results by spending compute on symbolic search rather than neural inference.

Architecture (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:282)

  • 8.0 Three-Lane Verification Architecture (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:399) - Lane A is the core search loop (proof_search.py → lean_interface.py).
  • 8.1 Navigational Modules (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:479) - Core functions: navigate(), spread(), get_anchor_set(), compute_idf(), batch_get_positions(). - Provides try_tactic(goal_state, tactic_text) -> TacticResult with success/failure and new goal states.
  • 3.4 Lane B: Axle Proof Audit Integration (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:607) - For successful Lane A proofs: auditor.verify() provides authoritative confirmation.
  • Experiment Cycle 2: Retrieval Comparison (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Results.md:192) - Purpose: Stream 1 core question -- does navigational retrieval match or exceed dense retrieval?
  • Per-Ablation PAB Comparison (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Results.md:339) - Key findings: - Navigation thesis (1 vs 2): ____ - Architecture thesis (1 vs 3): ____ - Ternary thesis (1 vs 6): ____ - 6-bank thesis (1 vs 13): ____ - Spreading thesis (1 vs 4): ____

Architecture

1. Design Thesis (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:9)

  • Navigation, not prediction. The central design decision in Wayfinder is that proof search should be spatial navigation through structured mathematical space rather than sequential token prediction.
  • This design is directly adapted from ModelAtlas (Vinaik, 2025), which demonstrated that structured navigation over signed semantic coordinates outperforms both flat database queries and dense embedding retrieval for finding ML models on HuggingFace.
  • If tactic classification outperforms navigational coordinates (Phase 4.4 ablation), the ternary decoder design is wrong.

8.0 Three-Lane Verification Architecture (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:399)

  • Lane A is the core search loop (proof_search.py → lean_interface.py).

8.1 Navigational Modules (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:479)

  • Core functions: navigate(), spread(), get_anchor_set(), compute_idf(), batch_get_positions().
  • Provides try_tactic(goal_state, tactic_text) -> TacticResult with success/failure and new goal states.

Hardware & Distribution Strategy (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:30)

  • Distribution pattern: - CPU-heavy extraction/analysis scripts are standalone workers with zero Wayfinder src/ imports - All data exchange via JSONL (one JSON object per line, UTF-8) - Workers support --resume (read output, build skip set, append) - File sync via parsync (parallel SSH, resumable, chunked) instead of scp - Merge results back into the main DB on the laptop
  • Key parallelism: While the laptop runs encoder evaluation (Phase 0.6, GPU-bound), macpro and homebridge run proof network extraction and anchor gap analysis (Phase 0.1-0.5, CPU-only).

3.4 Lane B: Axle Proof Audit Integration (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:607)

  • For successful Lane A proofs: auditor.verify() provides authoritative confirmation.

Experiment Cycle 2: Retrieval Comparison (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Results.md:192)

  • Purpose: Stream 1 core question -- does navigational retrieval match or exceed dense retrieval?

Per-Ablation PAB Comparison (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Results.md:339)

  • Key findings: - Navigation thesis (1 vs 2): ____ - Architecture thesis (1 vs 3): ____ - Ternary thesis (1 vs 6): ____ - 6-bank thesis (1 vs 13): ____ - Spreading thesis (1 vs 4): ____

2.4 Premise Retrieval as Structured Navigation (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:190)

  • Scoring mechanism — open design question: Pure multiplicative scoring (Π bank_score_i) is both the system's precision advantage and its fragility.
  • LeanDojo's extraction already provides this accessibility information.
  • Performance: Batch SQL replaces per-lemma neural forward passes.
  • Pre-filter by accessible premises AND required anchors (SQL HAVING), batch-fetch positions and anchor sets, score in Python from in-memory dicts.
  • Four indexed queries instead of 100k dot products.

Problem Solving (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:84)

  • 2.1 Proof Banks (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:23) - Why signed hierarchies instead of categories: A categorical "domain" field with values {algebra, topology, analysis} can't express proximity. - Signed positions give gradient scoring — a linear algebra lemma (DOMAIN = 0, between concrete and abstract) scores better against an algebra query than a topology lemma, without binary mismatch.
  • The Name* (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:591) - They navigated by understanding the structure* of the space, not by memorizing routes.
  • 5.1 Enriched Proof Network (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:702) - If navigational retrieval underperforms dense retrieval: - Add LLM-based anchor extraction (Tier 3) for subtle mathematical properties - Add cross-namespace links from proof co-occurrence analysis - Experiment with finer-grained bank positions (depth 3-4 instead of 1-2)
  • 1.5 Scope and Stance (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:85) - Our contribution is architectural: demonstrating that a small model (sub-100M trainable parameters) with structured navigation can achieve competitive results by spending compute on symbolic search rather than neural inference.
  • 3.9 Deterministic Lowering (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:664) - This is the existing lowering.py module, adapted to accept navigational resolution output instead of tier tokens.

Alignment (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:191)

  • Design Thesis (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:9) - Navigation, not prediction.* The central design decision in Wayfinder is that proof search should be spatial navigation through structured mathematical space rather than sequential token prediction*.
  • 2.1 Proof Banks (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:23) - Why signed hierarchies instead of categories: A categorical "domain" field with values {algebra, topology, analysis} can't express proximity.
  • 2.2 Anchor Dictionary (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:40) - For each failure (correct premise not in top-16), identify what anchors would have connected goal to premise.
  • 1.1 The Navigation Problem in Proof Search (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:29) - Every competitive neural theorem prover uses the same fundamental approach: encode the proof state as a dense vector, predict the next tactic token from a vocabulary, and embed the prediction in a tree search (BFS, MCTS, or best-first).
  • 1.5 Scope and Stance (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:85) - Non-goal: Beating DeepSeek-Prover-V2's 88.9% on MiniF2F. - Our contribution is architectural: demonstrating that a small model (sub-100M trainable parameters) with structured navigation can achieve competitive results by spending compute on symbolic search rather than neural inference.

Architecture (.lintgate/wiki/Theory-Profile.md:282)

  • 8.0 Three-Lane Verification Architecture (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:399) - Lane A is the core search loop (proof_search.py → lean_interface.py).
  • 8.1 Navigational Modules (.lintgate/wiki/Design.md:479) - Core functions: navigate(), spread(), get_anchor_set(), compute_idf(), batch_get_positions(). - Provides try_tactic(goal_state, tactic_text) -> TacticResult with success/failure and new goal states.
  • 3.4 Lane B: Axle Proof Audit Integration (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:607) - For successful Lane A proofs: auditor.verify() provides authoritative confirmation.
  • Experiment Cycle 2: Retrieval Comparison (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Results.md:192) - Purpose: Stream 1 core question -- does navigational retrieval match or exceed dense retrieval?
  • Per-Ablation PAB Comparison (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Results.md:339) - Key findings: - Navigation thesis (1 vs 2): ____ - Architecture thesis (1 vs 3): ____ - Ternary thesis (1 vs 6): ____ - 6-bank thesis (1 vs 13): ____ - Spreading thesis (1 vs 4): ____

Anti Patterns

3.2 Goal Encoder (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:469)

  • Its byte-level operation handles Lean's Unicode symbols (∀, ⊢, →, ℕ, ⋃) without tokenization artifacts — a critical advantage over subword tokenizers that fragment mathematical notation.
  • Since Wayfinder already uses ternary weights in the decoder (TernaryLinear with STE), extending ternary quantization to the encoder would be architecturally consistent and could enable real-time premise retrieval.
  • Recent work on structured pruning demonstrates that ~95-99% of nodes can be removed from large generalist models while retaining domain-specific performance, especially when followed by brief fine-tuning on in-domain data.
  • Key design constraint: The encoder may be frozen or fine-tuned, but the downstream architecture (GoalAnalyzer, Bridge, Navigator) must work with either.

Abstractions

Bootstrap ~200 anchors from mathematical vocabulary (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:102)

  • Output: data/proof_network.db` — SQLite database.
  • Validation: - All ~100k Mathlib lemmas inserted as entities - Bank positions assigned to ≥95% of entities (deterministic extraction from types/namespaces) - ≥3 anchors per entity on average - IDF computed and cached - Unit tests for navigate(), spread(), and scoring functions
  • Compute: Local, CPU-only, <2 hours.

3.3 Benchmark Evaluation (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:580)

  • What: Evaluate on standard benchmarks.
  • Benchmarks: 1. MiniF2F-test (488 problems): Primary comparison benchmark. 2. Mathlib test split (~2k held-out theorems): Larger-scale evaluation.
  • Metrics: - Theorems proved (within search budget) - Average search budget consumed per proof - Neural forward passes per proof (should be dramatically lower than baselines) - Wall-clock time per proof
  • Baselines for comparison: - ReProver (published numbers) - LeanProgress (published numbers) - DeepSeek-Prover-V2 (published numbers, noting parameter count difference) - Ablated Wayfinder variants (from Phase 2 ablations)

4.3 Stream 3 Analysis: PAB Trajectory Evaluation (.lintgate/wiki/Experiment-Plan.md:662)

  • Questions to answer: - Does navigational accuracy trajectory predict final proof success better than loss trajectory? - Does crystallization rate predict navigational consistency? - Does progress prediction accuracy improve monotonically?

2.3 Anchor Dictionaries as Mathematical Vocabulary (.lintgate/wiki/Research.md:151)

  • An anchor is a semantic label shared across mathematical entities.
  • This mirrors how human mathematicians search: they key off the unusual, distinctive features of a problem.
  • Anchor gap analysis (critical bootstrap step): The ~300 initial anchors will have blind spots.
  • - Type coercion anchors: Many Mathlib proofs struggle with coercion between types (↑n, (n : ℤ), Nat.cast).
  • This is a major source of proof difficulty and needs dedicated anchors: needs-cast, nat-int-coercion, subtype-coercion, coe-simp. - Algebraic hierarchy anchors: The Mathlib typeclass hierarchy (Monoid → Group → Ring → Field) creates proof obligations that are structurally similar but domain-specific.

See Also

  • Phase-5
  • Theory-Compass
Project Compass →

Generated by LintGate
